name: U.S. History
description: A survey of United States history from colonization through the modern era — covering founding ideals, expansion, Civil War, industrialization, the World Wars, the Cold War, and contemporary America.

modules.title: Colonial America & the Road to Revolution
modules.titleZh: 殖民地美洲与革命之路
modules.objectives: Describe the settlement of the thirteen colonies; analyze colonial life and the causes of the American Revolution.
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #3 — The Colonial Era
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Colonial America reading & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the economies and social structures of the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies. Which regional differences would later contribute to conflicts in American history?

modules.title: The American Revolution & Founding Documents
modules.objectives: Explain the causes and key events of the Revolution; analyze the Declaration of Independence and Articles of Confederation.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — The American Revolution 1763–1783
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #7 — Who Won the American Revolution?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — American Revolution article & questions
modules.assignment: Analyze the Declaration of Independence. Identify three grievances listed by Jefferson and explain how each connects to Enlightenment ideas about natural rights.

modules.title: The Constitution & Bill of Rights
modules.objectives: Describe the Constitutional Convention; explain the three branches of government, checks and balances, and the Bill of Rights.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Creating a New Government 1776–1790s
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #8 — The Constitution, the Articles, and Federalism
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Constitution article & questions
modules.assignment: Explain how the system of checks and balances prevents any one branch of government from becoming too powerful. Give one historical or contemporary example for each check.

modules.title: Westward Expansion & Manifest Destiny
modules.objectives: Trace U.S. expansion from the Louisiana Purchase to the Pacific; analyze the impact on Native Americans and Mexicans.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Westward Expansion and Reform 1815–1880
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #21 — Westward Expansion
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Manifest Destiny article & questions
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word essay from two perspectives — a white settler and a Cherokee person — on the forced removal known as the Trail of Tears. What did Manifest Destiny mean to each?

modules.title: Slavery & the Road to Civil War
modules.objectives: Explain how slavery expanded and became politically contested; analyze key events leading to Southern secession.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Civil War and Reconstruction
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Causes of the Civil War
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #20 — The Road to the Civil War
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Causes of the Civil War article & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the Missouri Compromise (1820), Compromise of 1850, and Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854). How did each attempt to resolve the slavery crisis, and why did each fail?

modules.title: The Civil War
modules.objectives: Identify the causes, major turning-point battles, and consequences of the Civil War; analyze the Emancipation Proclamation.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — A House Divided: The Civil War
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #20 — The Civil War Part I
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Civil War overview & questions
modules.assignment: Analyze the Emancipation Proclamation. What did it actually free, and what were its military, political, and moral significance?

modules.title: Reconstruction & the Gilded Age
modules.objectives: Explain Reconstruction policies and their failure; describe industrialization and the rise of big business.
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #22 — Reconstruction and 1876
modules.assignment: Explain how the Compromise of 1877 effectively ended Reconstruction. What were the long-term consequences for African Americans in the South?

modules.title: Immigration, Urbanization & Progressivism
modules.titleZh: 移民、城市化与进步主义
modules.objectives: Describe the waves of immigration and urban growth; analyze Progressive Era reforms and the women's suffrage movement.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Progressive Era to New Era 1900–1929
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #27 — The Progressive Era
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Progressive Era article & questions
modules.assignment: Choose one muckraker (e.g., Upton Sinclair, Ida Tarbell, Jacob Riis). Describe what they exposed and what reform their work helped produce.

modules.title: World War I & the 1920s
modules.titleZh: 第一次世界大战与二十年代
modules.objectives: Explain U.S. entry into WWI; analyze the Treaty of Versailles, the Roaring Twenties, and the Harlem Renaissance.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — World War I
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The U.S. in World War I
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #30 — World War I
modules.assignment: Why did the U.S. Senate refuse to ratify the Treaty of Versailles? Evaluate Wilson's Fourteen Points and the argument of the isolationists.

modules.title: The Great Depression & New Deal
modules.objectives: Explain the causes of the Great Depression; analyze how FDR's New Deal programs responded to the economic crisis.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Great Depression and WWII 1929–1945
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #33 — The Great Depression
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Great Depression article & questions
modules.assignment: Evaluate the New Deal: Choose two specific programs (e.g., CCC, Social Security Act, FDIC) and explain how each addressed a specific problem caused by the Depression.

modules.title: World War II
modules.objectives: Trace U.S. involvement from neutrality to Pearl Harbor; analyze the war effort, the Holocaust, and the decision to use the atomic bomb.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — WWII Primary Sources
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The U.S. in World War II
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #35 — World War II Part 1
modules.assignment: Was the U.S. decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki justified? Write a 350-word argument citing at least two pieces of historical evidence on each side.

modules.title: The Cold War & Korean/Vietnam Wars
modules.objectives: Explain containment policy; trace Cold War conflicts from Korea and Vietnam to McCarthyism and the arms race.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Post-War United States 1945–1968
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #37 — The Cold War
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Cold War article & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the Korean War and the Vietnam War as examples of U.S. containment policy. In what ways did each war succeed or fail in its Cold War objectives?

modules.title: The Civil Rights Movement
modules.objectives: Trace key events of the Civil Rights Movement from Brown v. Board to the Civil Rights Act; analyze strategies of nonviolent protest.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Civil Rights Movement
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Civil Rights Movement Overview
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #39 — The Civil Rights Movement
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Civil Rights article & questions
modules.assignment: Compare the philosophies of Martin Luther King Jr. and Malcolm X. What methods did each advocate, and how did their views evolve over time?

modules.title: America Since 1980
modules.objectives: Describe the Reagan Revolution and end of the Cold War; analyze post-9/11 America, globalization, and contemporary political debates.
modules.readingNote: Library of Congress — Contemporary United States 1968–Present
modules.video2Note: Crash Course US History #43 — The Reagan Revolution
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Reagan Era article & questions
modules.assignment: How did the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks change American foreign policy, civil liberties, and national identity? Discuss at least three specific consequences.

questions.question: The Mayflower Compact (1620) is significant because it:
options: Declared independence from Britain
Established the first written self-government agreement in the colonies
Created the House of Burgesses
Granted religious freedom to all colonists
questions.answer: Established the first written self-government agreement in the colonies

questions.question: The phrase "no taxation without representation" referred to colonists' objection to:
options: High import tariffs on luxury goods
British laws taxing the colonies without colonial representation in Parliament
Paying for the French and Indian War
British troops living in colonial homes
questions.answer: British laws taxing the colonies without colonial representation in Parliament

questions.question: The opening line of the Declaration of Independence — "We hold these truths to be self-evident" — draws most directly from:
options: The Magna Carta
The English Bill of Rights
Enlightenment philosophy, particularly John Locke
Puritan theology
questions.answer: Enlightenment philosophy, particularly John Locke

questions.question: The Articles of Confederation failed primarily because they:
options: Gave the federal government too much power over states
Did not create a president
Gave Congress no power to tax or regulate commerce
Required a unanimous vote to declare war
questions.answer: Gave Congress no power to tax or regulate commerce

questions.question: Which Constitutional principle divides power between the national and state governments?
options: Separation of powers
Federalism
Judicial review
Popular sovereignty
questions.answer: Federalism

questions.question: The Louisiana Purchase (1803) roughly doubled U.S. territory and was made from:
options: Spain
Britain
France (Napoleon)
Mexico
questions.answer: France (Napoleon)

questions.question: The Trail of Tears (1838–39) refers to:
options: The migration of settlers along the Oregon Trail
The forced relocation of the Cherokee Nation from Georgia to Oklahoma
The retreat of the U.S. Army after the Battle of Little Bighorn
Wagon trains crossing the Sierra Nevada mountains
questions.answer: The forced relocation of the Cherokee Nation from Georgia to Oklahoma

questions.question: The Missouri Compromise (1820) attempted to resolve the slavery debate by:
options: Abolishing slavery in all new territories
Admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, with a dividing line at 36°30'
Letting each territory vote on slavery
Compensating slave owners for freeing enslaved people
questions.answer: Admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, with a dividing line at 36°30'

questions.question: The Emancipation Proclamation (1863) declared freedom for enslaved people:
options: In all U.S. states immediately
Only in Confederate states still in rebellion
In border states loyal to the Union
Only after the war ended
questions.answer: Only in Confederate states still in rebellion

questions.question: Which battle is considered the turning point of the Civil War, halting the Confederacy's northern advance?
options: Bull Run
Antietam
Gettysburg
Vicksburg
questions.answer: Gettysburg

questions.question: The 13th Amendment to the Constitution (1865) abolished:
options: Racial segregation
Slavery throughout the United States
Poll taxes
Literacy tests for voting
questions.answer: Slavery throughout the United States

questions.question: The term "Robber Barons" refers to:
options: Corrupt politicians of the Gilded Age
Powerful industrial capitalists who monopolized industries
European nobility who invested in U.S. railroads
Outlaw gangs in the American West
questions.answer: Powerful industrial capitalists who monopolized industries

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'popular sovereignty' and describe how it contributed to the violence known as 'Bleeding Kansas' in the 1850s.
questions.answer: Popular sovereignty meant that settlers in a new territory would vote to decide whether slavery would be permitted. The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) applied this principle to Kansas, drawing pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers into the territory to influence the vote. The resulting competition led to ballot fraud, attacks, and guerrilla warfare between the two factions, earning the territory the name 'Bleeding Kansas' and demonstrating that popular sovereignty could not peacefully resolve the slavery question.

questions.question: The Homestead Act of 1862 encouraged western settlement by:
options: Paying settlers a cash bounty for moving west
Offering 160 acres of public land to settlers who would farm it for five years
Providing free railroad tickets to the frontier
Allowing purchase of Native American lands at low prices
questions.answer: Offering 160 acres of public land to settlers who would farm it for five years

questions.question: What were the three Reconstruction Amendments (13th, 14th, 15th), and why did Reconstruction ultimately fail to deliver lasting equality for African Americans?
questions.answer: The 13th Amendment abolished slavery, the 14th Amendment guaranteed equal citizenship and due process, and the 15th Amendment prohibited denying the right to vote based on race. Reconstruction failed because of the Compromise of 1877, which withdrew federal troops from the South; the rise of the Ku Klux Klan and white supremacist violence; the emergence of Black Codes and later Jim Crow laws; sharecropping systems that kept freedpeople in economic servitude; and the Supreme Court's narrow interpretation of the 14th Amendment that permitted segregation.

questions.question: The Boston Tea Party (1773) was a protest against:
options: A tax on all colonial exports
The Tea Act, which gave the British East India Company a monopoly on tea sales
Britain's refusal to allow colonial tea production
Forced quartering of British soldiers
questions.answer: The Tea Act, which gave the British East India Company a monopoly on tea sales

questions.question: The Great Compromise at the Constitutional Convention created:
options: The Electoral College and popular vote
A bicameral legislature with a Senate (equal state representation) and House (proportional representation)
The Three-Fifths Compromise for enslaved people
A strong executive with veto power
questions.answer: A bicameral legislature with a Senate (equal state representation) and House (proportional representation)

questions.question: Manifest Destiny expressed the belief that:
options: America should remain isolated from European affairs
The United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent
Native Americans should be assimilated into U.S. culture
The federal government should control all western lands
questions.answer: The United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent

questions.question: The Dred Scott v. Sandford decision (1857) ruled that:
options: Slavery was unconstitutional in northern states
African Americans were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal courts
Congress could ban slavery in territories
Enslaved people who reached free states were automatically free
questions.answer: African Americans were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal courts

questions.question: The Civil War began when Confederate forces attacked:
options: Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Fort Sumter, South Carolina
Washington, D.C.
Richmond, Virginia
questions.answer: Fort Sumter, South Carolina

questions.question: Muckrakers were Progressive Era journalists who:
options: Supported big business and opposed government regulation
Investigated and exposed corruption, unsafe conditions, and social injustices
Covered foreign policy and diplomacy
Wrote propaganda supporting U.S. entry into WWI
questions.answer: Investigated and exposed corruption, unsafe conditions, and social injustices

questions.question: The Zimmermann Telegram (1917) helped push the U.S. into WWI by:
options: Promising Germany's alliance with Mexico against the United States
Revealing Germany's plan to sink American ships
Offering peace terms to the Allied powers
Declaring war on the United States
questions.answer: Promising Germany's alliance with Mexico against the United States

questions.question: The stock market crash of October 1929 contributed to the Great Depression by:
options: Causing a natural drought that destroyed crops
Wiping out investors' savings and triggering bank failures and credit contraction
Leading to a global trade war that eliminated American exports overnight
Causing the federal government to raise taxes sharply
questions.answer: Wiping out investors' savings and triggering bank failures and credit contraction

questions.question: The Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) resulted in:
options: The U.S. declaring war only on Japan
The U.S. formally entering WWII against Japan, Germany, and Italy
Germany surrendering within months
A U.S.-Soviet military alliance against Japan
questions.answer: The U.S. formally entering WWII against Japan, Germany, and Italy

questions.question: The policy of containment, as articulated by George Kennan and the Truman Doctrine, aimed to:
options: Eliminate communism worldwide through military force
Prevent the further spread of Soviet communist influence beyond its existing borders
Negotiate arms reduction with the Soviet Union
Promote democracy in Western Europe only
questions.answer: Prevent the further spread of Soviet communist influence beyond its existing borders

questions.question: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned the precedent set by:
options: Dred Scott v. Sandford
Marbury v. Madison
Plessy v. Ferguson
McCulloch v. Maryland
questions.answer: Plessy v. Ferguson

questions.question: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibited discrimination based on:
options: Race only
Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
Race and religion only
Age and disability
questions.answer: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin

questions.question: Reaganomics (supply-side economics) was based on the idea that:
options: Government spending should increase to stimulate demand
Tax cuts for corporations and the wealthy would stimulate investment and growth that benefits everyone
Tariffs on imports protect American jobs
The federal deficit should be eliminated immediately
questions.answer: Tax cuts for corporations and the wealthy would stimulate investment and growth that benefits everyone

questions.question: The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolized:
options: The beginning of the Cold War
Germany's defeat in WWII
The collapse of communist governments in Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War
The reunification of Germany under communist rule
questions.answer: The collapse of communist governments in Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War

questions.question: The USA PATRIOT Act (2001), passed after 9/11, was controversial because:
options: It declared war on Afghanistan without Congressional approval
Critics argued it expanded government surveillance powers in ways that threatened civil liberties
It authorized the use of nuclear weapons
It created a national ID card system
questions.answer: Critics argued it expanded government surveillance powers in ways that threatened civil liberties

questions.question: The internment of Japanese Americans during WWII was authorized by:
options: The Espionage Act
Executive Order 9066
The Alien and Sedition Acts
A Supreme Court ruling
questions.answer: Executive Order 9066

questions.question: The Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–56) was triggered by:
options: A federal court ruling desegregating public transit
Rosa Parks' arrest for refusing to give up her seat to a white passenger
The assassination of Medgar Evers
Martin Luther King Jr.'s "I Have a Dream" speech
questions.answer: Rosa Parks' arrest for refusing to give up her seat to a white passenger

questions.question: Describe the Marshall Plan and explain why it was both a humanitarian and a strategic Cold War decision for the United States.
questions.answer: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided over $13 billion in U.S. economic aid to rebuild war-devastated Western European nations after WWII. Humanitarianly, it fed starving populations and rebuilt destroyed infrastructure. Strategically, the U.S. feared that economic desperation would make Western European nations vulnerable to communist political movements and Soviet influence — as had already occurred in Eastern Europe. By rebuilding strong, prosperous democracies, the U.S. created stable trading partners and reliable Cold War allies, while demonstrating that capitalism and democracy could outperform communism at improving living standards.

questions.question: How did the Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s employ nonviolent direct action, and what were two major legislative achievements that resulted from this activism?
questions.answer: Civil rights activists used nonviolent direct action tactics inspired by Mahatma Gandhi, including sit-ins (like the Greensboro sit-ins of 1960), freedom rides on interstate buses, marches (like the March on Washington in 1963 and the Selma-to-Montgomery marches of 1965), and economic boycotts. These tactics exposed the brutality of segregation to national and international audiences, building public pressure for change. Two major legislative achievements were the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which banned discrimination in public accommodations and employment, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which prohibited discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests that had disenfranchised Black voters across the South.

questions.question: How did globalization from the 1990s onward reshape the American economy, and what political tensions did these changes produce?
questions.answer: Globalization accelerated through trade agreements like NAFTA (1994) and the WTO, the rise of the internet, and containerized shipping. It integrated the U.S. economy into global supply chains, lowering consumer prices and expanding export markets. However, it also led to manufacturing job losses as companies moved production to lower-wage countries, contributing to deindustrialization in the Rust Belt. Growing economic inequality between college-educated workers who benefited and working-class communities that lost factory jobs produced significant political tension — fueling trade protectionist sentiment, debates about immigration, and the political polarization that characterized early 21st-century American politics.

quizQuestions.question: The Jamestown settlement (1607) was founded primarily for:
options: Religious freedom
Economic profit by the Virginia Company
Escaping political persecution
Military strategic purposes
quizQuestions.answer: Economic profit by the Virginia Company

quizQuestions.question: The Mayflower Compact is historically significant because it:
options: Declared independence from England
Established the first example of self-government by agreement in the colonies
Created the first colonial legislature
Granted land rights to Pilgrim settlers
quizQuestions.answer: Established the first example of self-government by agreement in the colonies

quizQuestions.question: The Southern colonies' economy was primarily based on:
options: Manufacturing and trade
Fishing and shipbuilding
Cash crop plantation agriculture using enslaved labor
Small subsistence farming
quizQuestions.answer: Cash crop plantation agriculture using enslaved labor

quizQuestions.question: The French and Indian War (1754–63) resulted in Britain:
options: Losing its American colonies
Gaining control of French Canada but accumulating large debts
Forming an alliance with Native American tribes
Withdrawing from North America entirely
quizQuestions.answer: Gaining control of French Canada but accumulating large debts

quizQuestions.question: The Proclamation of 1763 angered many colonists because it:
options: Imposed a new tax on colonial imports
Forbade colonists from settling west of the Appalachian Mountains
Required colonists to house British soldiers
Dissolved colonial assemblies
quizQuestions.answer: Forbade colonists from settling west of the Appalachian Mountains

quizQuestions.question: Thomas Paine's 'Common Sense' (1776) argued that:
options: The colonies should seek reconciliation with Britain
A monarchy was the best form of government
American independence was necessary and a republic was superior to monarchy
The Continental Army needed more funding
quizQuestions.answer: American independence was necessary and a republic was superior to monarchy

quizQuestions.question: The Battle of Saratoga (1777) was a turning point in the Revolution because it:
options: Captured the British capital of New York City
Convinced France to formally ally with the Americans
Led directly to the British surrender at Yorktown
Was the first American military victory
quizQuestions.answer: Convinced France to formally ally with the Americans

quizQuestions.question: The Articles of Confederation created a central government that:
options: Could tax citizens directly
Had a powerful executive branch
Relied on states to voluntarily provide funds and soldiers
Controlled interstate commerce
quizQuestions.answer: Relied on states to voluntarily provide funds and soldiers

quizQuestions.question: The Declaration of Independence was primarily authored by:
options: Benjamin Franklin
John Adams
Thomas Jefferson
George Washington
quizQuestions.answer: Thomas Jefferson

quizQuestions.question: The American Revolution was finalized with the Treaty of Paris (1783), in which Britain recognized:
options: American independence and U.S. territory east of the Mississippi River
American independence with territory limited to the original 13 colonies
A British protectorate over the new United States
Joint British-American governance of the Ohio Valley
quizQuestions.answer: American independence and U.S. territory east of the Mississippi River

quizQuestions.question: The Constitutional Convention took place in:
options: Boston
New York City
Philadelphia
Washington, D.C.
quizQuestions.answer: Philadelphia

quizQuestions.question: The system of checks and balances ensures that:
options: The president appoints all federal judges
Each branch of government can limit the power of the other branches
Congress has final authority over all laws
The Supreme Court can override any election result
quizQuestions.answer: Each branch of government can limit the power of the other branches

quizQuestions.question: The First Amendment to the Constitution protects:
options: The right to bear arms
Freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition
Protection against unreasonable searches
The right to a fair trial
quizQuestions.answer: Freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition

quizQuestions.question: The Federalist Papers were written to:
options: Argue against ratifying the Constitution
Persuade New York to ratify the Constitution
Propose amendments to the Bill of Rights
Explain the failings of the Articles of Confederation only
quizQuestions.answer: Persuade New York to ratify the Constitution

quizQuestions.question: The Three-Fifths Compromise counted each enslaved person as:
options: A full person for representation and taxation
Three-fifths of a person for both representation and taxation
Not counted at all for any purpose
A full person for taxation but not representation
quizQuestions.answer: Three-fifths of a person for both representation and taxation

quizQuestions.question: President Thomas Jefferson purchased the Louisiana Territory in 1803 from:
options: Britain
Spain
France
Mexico
quizQuestions.answer: France

quizQuestions.question: The expedition of Lewis and Clark (1804–06) was sent to:
options: Establish military forts in the West
Explore the new Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific
Negotiate treaties with Native American tribes
Map the Rocky Mountains for future railroads
quizQuestions.answer: Explore the new Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific

quizQuestions.question: The Indian Removal Act (1830) was signed by President:
options: Thomas Jefferson
James Monroe
Andrew Jackson
James K. Polk
quizQuestions.answer: Andrew Jackson

quizQuestions.question: The Mexican-American War (1846–48) resulted in the U.S. acquiring:
options: Texas only
California, New Mexico, and other territories (the Mexican Cession)
The entire Mexican nation
Cuba and Puerto Rico
quizQuestions.answer: California, New Mexico, and other territories (the Mexican Cession)

quizQuestions.question: The Oregon Trail was primarily used by:
options: Fur traders seeking beaver pelts
Settlers migrating from the East to the Pacific Northwest
U.S. Army supply convoys
Pony Express mail riders
quizQuestions.answer: Settlers migrating from the East to the Pacific Northwest

quizQuestions.question: Harriet Beecher Stowe's novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin' (1852) contributed to the Civil War by:
options: Providing military strategy to the Union Army
Strengthening the abolitionist movement by portraying the horrors of slavery
Convincing Southern states to secede
Helping elect Abraham Lincoln as president
quizQuestions.answer: Strengthening the abolitionist movement by portraying the horrors of slavery

quizQuestions.question: The Dred Scott decision (1857) declared that:
options: Slavery was legal only in the South
Enslaved people were property, not citizens, with no right to sue in court
Congress had power to ban slavery in territories
Free states must return escaped enslaved people
quizQuestions.answer: Enslaved people were property, not citizens, with no right to sue in court

quizQuestions.question: The primary stated cause of Southern secession in 1860–61 was:
options: Tariff disputes
The preservation of slavery and states' rights
Economic competition with Northern industry
Disagreement over westward expansion routes
quizQuestions.answer: The preservation of slavery and states' rights

quizQuestions.question: John Brown's 1859 raid on Harpers Ferry aimed to:
options: Assassinate President Buchanan
Seize federal weapons to spark a slave rebellion
Protest the Kansas-Nebraska Act
Establish a free state in Virginia
quizQuestions.answer: Seize federal weapons to spark a slave rebellion

quizQuestions.question: The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) effectively repealed the:
options: Dred Scott decision
Missouri Compromise
Compromise of 1850
Northwest Ordinance
quizQuestions.answer: Missouri Compromise

quizQuestions.question: The Civil War began in April 1861 when Confederate forces attacked:
options: Washington, D.C.
Fort Sumter in South Carolina
Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Manassas, Virginia
quizQuestions.answer: Fort Sumter in South Carolina

quizQuestions.question: The Emancipation Proclamation took effect on:
options: January 1, 1863
July 4, 1862
April 9, 1865
December 6, 1865
quizQuestions.answer: January 1, 1863

quizQuestions.question: General Ulysses S. Grant is most associated with:
options: Leading Confederate forces in Virginia
The Union strategy of total war and victory in the western theater
Defending Fort Sumter
The Union blockade of Confederate ports
quizQuestions.answer: The Union strategy of total war and victory in the western theater

quizQuestions.question: The Battle of Gettysburg (July 1–3, 1863) was significant because:
options: It was the first major Confederate defeat
It halted Lee's invasion of the North and shifted momentum to the Union
It resulted in the capture of Richmond, Virginia
It opened the Mississippi River to Union control
quizQuestions.answer: It halted Lee's invasion of the North and shifted momentum to the Union

quizQuestions.question: The Civil War officially ended when General Robert E. Lee surrendered at:
options: Gettysburg, Pennsylvania
Richmond, Virginia
Appomattox Court House, Virginia
Fort Sumter, South Carolina
quizQuestions.answer: Appomattox Court House, Virginia

quizQuestions.question: The 14th Amendment (1868) granted:
options: The right to vote to all male citizens
Citizenship and equal protection under the law to all persons born in the U.S.
The abolition of slavery
Women the right to vote
quizQuestions.answer: Citizenship and equal protection under the law to all persons born in the U.S.

quizQuestions.question: Sharecropping kept many freedpeople in poverty after the Civil War because:
options: It paid lower wages than factory work
Sharecroppers often fell into debt to landowners and could not leave
The federal government taxed sharecroppers at higher rates
It was illegal in most Southern states
quizQuestions.answer: Sharecroppers often fell into debt to landowners and could not leave

quizQuestions.question: The Transcontinental Railroad (completed 1869) was significant because it:
options: Connected Canada and Mexico for the first time
Linked the Atlantic and Pacific coasts, transforming commerce and settlement
Was built entirely by the federal government
Replaced the Mississippi River as the main trade route
quizQuestions.answer: Linked the Atlantic and Pacific coasts, transforming commerce and settlement

quizQuestions.question: Social Darwinism, used to justify the wealth of Robber Barons, held that:
options: Governments should redistribute wealth to the poor
The rich deserved their wealth as proof of natural superiority (survival of the fittest)
Workers should form unions to balance corporate power
All citizens deserved equal economic opportunity
quizQuestions.answer: The rich deserved their wealth as proof of natural superiority (survival of the fittest)

quizQuestions.question: The Compromise of 1877 ended Reconstruction by:
options: Passing the Civil Rights Act
Giving the presidency to Rutherford Hayes in exchange for withdrawing federal troops from the South
Granting full citizenship rights to freedpeople in Southern states
Abolishing the Freedmen's Bureau
quizQuestions.answer: Giving the presidency to Rutherford Hayes in exchange for withdrawing federal troops from the South

quizQuestions.question: The largest wave of immigration to the United States (1880–1920) came primarily from:
options: Northern and Western Europe
Southern and Eastern Europe, as well as Asia
Latin America and the Caribbean
Canada and Australia
quizQuestions.answer: Southern and Eastern Europe, as well as Asia

quizQuestions.question: Upton Sinclair's novel 'The Jungle' (1906) exposed:
options: Political corruption in city governments
Unsafe and unsanitary conditions in the meatpacking industry
The exploitation of child miners in Appalachia
Racial discrimination in Southern sharecropping
quizQuestions.answer: Unsafe and unsanitary conditions in the meatpacking industry

quizQuestions.question: The 19th Amendment (1920) granted:
options: The right to vote to all male citizens regardless of race
Women the right to vote
Prohibition of alcohol
Income tax authorization to Congress
quizQuestions.answer: Women the right to vote

quizQuestions.question: The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) was designed to:
options: Protect labor unions from prosecution
Break up monopolies and promote business competition
Regulate railroad rates
Establish a national banking system
quizQuestions.answer: Break up monopolies and promote business competition

quizQuestions.question: Settlement houses like Hull House, founded by Jane Addams, provided:
options: Free land grants to immigrant farmers
Community services and social support to urban immigrants and the poor
Job placement for industrial workers
English language instruction to foreign diplomats
quizQuestions.answer: Community services and social support to urban immigrants and the poor

quizQuestions.question: The sinking of the Lusitania (1915) contributed to growing U.S. support for entering WWI because:
options: It was a U.S. Navy vessel
Almost 1,200 people including 128 Americans were killed by a German submarine
It carried secret U.S. military supplies to Britain
It triggered a British blockade of U.S. ports
quizQuestions.answer: Almost 1,200 people including 128 Americans were killed by a German submarine

quizQuestions.question: Wilson's Fourteen Points proposed:
options: A harsh peace treaty punishing Germany for the war
A framework for lasting peace including a League of Nations
U.S. expansion into former German colonies
Reparations from all Central Powers nations
quizQuestions.answer: A framework for lasting peace including a League of Nations

quizQuestions.question: The Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s was primarily:
options: A political movement for civil rights legislation
A flourishing of African American art, literature, and music centered in New York City
A migration of African Americans from the North to the South
A religious revival movement in urban African American communities
quizQuestions.answer: A flourishing of African American art, literature, and music centered in New York City

quizQuestions.question: Prohibition (18th Amendment, 1920) banned:
options: The sale of cigarettes
The manufacture, sale, and transportation of alcoholic beverages
Public consumption of alcohol only
Importing alcohol from foreign countries
quizQuestions.answer: The manufacture, sale, and transportation of alcoholic beverages

quizQuestions.question: The Roaring Twenties were characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Economic prosperity and consumer culture
Jazz music and changing social norms
Widespread economic equality between rich and poor
Mass production of automobiles
quizQuestions.answer: Widespread economic equality between rich and poor

quizQuestions.question: The stock market crashed on 'Black Tuesday' in:
options: October 1927
October 1929
October 1931
October 1933
quizQuestions.answer: October 1929

quizQuestions.question: The Dust Bowl of the 1930s was caused by:
options: Industrial pollution of farmland
Severe drought combined with poor farming practices that stripped topsoil from the Great Plains
A federal policy that paid farmers to destroy crops
Flooding of the Mississippi River valley
quizQuestions.answer: Severe drought combined with poor farming practices that stripped topsoil from the Great Plains

quizQuestions.question: FDR's New Deal was primarily intended to:
options: Declare war on Germany before WWII officially began
Provide relief, recovery, and reform to address the Great Depression
Reduce the size of the federal government
Eliminate all private businesses in favor of government control
quizQuestions.answer: Provide relief, recovery, and reform to address the Great Depression

quizQuestions.question: The Social Security Act (1935) established:
options: Universal healthcare for all Americans
A federal program providing retirement income and unemployment insurance
Free housing for unemployed workers
Government ownership of banks
quizQuestions.answer: A federal program providing retirement income and unemployment insurance

quizQuestions.question: Hoovervilles, the shantytowns that appeared during the Great Depression, were named after President Hoover because:
options: He funded their construction as relief housing
Many Americans blamed his policies for the Depression
He lived in a similar structure to show solidarity
They were built on land donated by the Hoover administration
quizQuestions.answer: Many Americans blamed his policies for the Depression

quizQuestions.question: Japan attacked the U.S. naval base at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii on:
options: December 7, 1941
September 1, 1939
June 6, 1944
August 6, 1945
quizQuestions.answer: December 7, 1941

quizQuestions.question: Executive Order 9066 (1942) authorized:
options: The use of atomic weapons against Japan
The forced internment of Japanese Americans in detention camps
The military draft for WWII
Rationing of food and consumer goods on the home front
quizQuestions.answer: The forced internment of Japanese Americans in detention camps

quizQuestions.question: D-Day (June 6, 1944) refers to:
options: The U.S. declaration of war on Germany
The Allied invasion of Nazi-occupied France at Normandy
Germany's unconditional surrender
The liberation of the Auschwitz concentration camp
quizQuestions.answer: The Allied invasion of Nazi-occupied France at Normandy

quizQuestions.question: The Holocaust resulted in the murder of approximately:
options: 1 million people, mostly political prisoners
6 million Jews and millions of others including Roma, disabled people, and political opponents
600,000 people across occupied Europe
10 million Soviet prisoners of war
quizQuestions.answer: 6 million Jews and millions of others including Roma, disabled people, and political opponents

quizQuestions.question: President Truman's decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945) was justified by the argument that:
options: Japan had already agreed to surrender
It would end the war quickly and avoid a costly land invasion of Japan
The Soviet Union had requested the bombing
It would destroy Japan's nuclear weapons program
quizQuestions.answer: It would end the war quickly and avoid a costly land invasion of Japan

quizQuestions.question: The Truman Doctrine (1947) promised U.S. support to:
options: All countries in economic need regardless of political system
Nations threatened by communist takeover, beginning with Greece and Turkey
Western European nations rebuilding after WWII
Latin American nations threatened by Soviet influence
quizQuestions.answer: Nations threatened by communist takeover, beginning with Greece and Turkey

quizQuestions.question: McCarthyism refers to:
options: The foreign policy of containment developed by George Kennan
Senator Joseph McCarthy's campaign of accusations and hearings targeting suspected communists
The U.S. military strategy in the Korean War
Espionage investigations into Soviet spies in the atomic program
quizQuestions.answer: Senator Joseph McCarthy's campaign of accusations and hearings targeting suspected communists

quizQuestions.question: The Korean War (1950–53) ended with:
options: A clear U.S./UN victory and Korean reunification
North Korea's complete defeat
An armistice that restored roughly the original border at the 38th parallel
China's conquest of the Korean peninsula
quizQuestions.answer: An armistice that restored roughly the original border at the 38th parallel

quizQuestions.question: The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) gave President Johnson authority to:
options: Declare war on North Vietnam through Congress
Escalate U.S. military involvement in Vietnam without a formal declaration of war
Use nuclear weapons in Southeast Asia
Negotiate a peace settlement with North Vietnam
quizQuestions.answer: Escalate U.S. military involvement in Vietnam without a formal declaration of war

quizQuestions.question: The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) was resolved when:
options: The U.S. invaded Cuba and destroyed the missile sites
The Soviet Union agreed to remove missiles from Cuba in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and removal of U.S. missiles from Turkey
Cuba voluntarily removed the missiles after UN negotiations
President Kennedy blockaded Cuba until Soviet ships turned back
quizQuestions.answer: The Soviet Union agreed to remove missiles from Cuba in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and removal of U.S. missiles from Turkey

quizQuestions.question: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) ruled that:
options: Affirmative action in college admissions was constitutional
Racial segregation in public schools was unconstitutional
Poll taxes violated the 14th Amendment
The Civil Rights Act of 1957 was legally valid
quizQuestions.answer: Racial segregation in public schools was unconstitutional

quizQuestions.question: The March on Washington (August 1963) is best known for:
options: Turning violent when police attacked peaceful marchers
Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech
Producing the first draft of the Civil Rights Act
Demanding an end to the Vietnam War
quizQuestions.answer: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech

quizQuestions.question: The strategy of nonviolent direct action used by civil rights activists was most influenced by:
options: Karl Marx's communist theory
Mahatma Gandhi's methods of civil disobedience in India
The Black Power movement's philosophy
President Eisenhower's moderate approach to civil rights
quizQuestions.answer: Mahatma Gandhi's methods of civil disobedience in India

quizQuestions.question: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was passed in response to:
options: Riots in northern cities
The assassination of Medgar Evers
The violent response of Alabama state troopers to marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma
Malcolm X's advocacy for Black voting rights
quizQuestions.answer: The violent response of Alabama state troopers to marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma

quizQuestions.question: The Black Power movement of the late 1960s differed from King's approach by:
options: Advocating for violent revolution against the U.S. government
Emphasizing Black self-determination, cultural pride, and community control rather than integration
Focusing exclusively on voter registration in the South
Rejecting all political activity in favor of economic boycotts
quizQuestions.answer: Emphasizing Black self-determination, cultural pride, and community control rather than integration

quizQuestions.question: President Reagan's economic policy (Reaganomics) centered on:
options: Raising taxes on the wealthy to fund social programs
Cutting taxes, reducing government regulation, and reducing social spending
Expanding the welfare state to reduce poverty
Nationalizing key industries to protect American workers
quizQuestions.answer: Cutting taxes, reducing government regulation, and reducing social spending

quizQuestions.question: The Berlin Wall fell in:
The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks were carried out by members of:
options: The Taliban government of Afghanistan
The Iraqi Republican Guard
Al-Qaeda, an Islamic extremist organization led by Osama bin Laden
Hezbollah, backed by Iran
quizQuestions.answer: Al-Qaeda, an Islamic extremist organization led by Osama bin Laden

quizQuestions.question: The USA PATRIOT Act (2001) expanded the government's ability to:
options: Deploy military forces abroad without Congressional approval
Conduct surveillance, intercept communications, and access financial records in terrorism investigations
Detain foreign nationals indefinitely without trial
Suspend habeas corpus for all U.S. citizens
quizQuestions.answer: Conduct surveillance, intercept communications, and access financial records in terrorism investigations

quizQuestions.question: Globalization in the post-Cold War era contributed to political tension in the U.S. primarily because:
options: It led to a military buildup that increased national debt
Manufacturing job losses in the Rust Belt created economic anxiety that fueled populist political movements
Foreign companies began buying U.S. government bonds in large quantities
Immigration from Latin America declined sharply, creating labor shortages
quizQuestions.answer: Manufacturing job losses in the Rust Belt created economic anxiety that fueled populist political movements
